rule.text: Wexler Mutual — Property Claims Handbook, §4.2 (Standing Deductible Rule). Unless a case-specific endorsement is on file, the deductible on every property claim is set at a flat two percent (2%) of the insured value recorded on the policy declarations page. Adjusters are reminded that the insured value, not the claimed loss amount, is the base for this calculation, and that the 2% figure is the standing rule for the current policy year.

Claim 105 has a deductible of $10,000 (2% of $500,000).
question: For each open claim below, state the deductible that applies under the Claims Handbook.

sources.text: Wexler Mutual — Property Claims Handbook, §4.2 (Standing Deductible Rule). Unless a case-specific endorsement is on file, the deductible on every property claim is set at a flat two percent (2%) of the insured value recorded on the policy declarations page. Adjusters are reminded that the insured value, not the claimed loss amount, is the base for this calculation, and that the 2% figure is the standing rule for the current policy year.

Claim 101 concerns storm damage to the roof of a detached house. The property is on the declarations page with an insured value of $400,000. The loss was reported within the notification window and the file is complete.
Claim 102 concerns water damage from a burst pipe in a townhouse. The declarations page records an insured value of $250,000 for the property. The claim documentation has passed intake review.
Claim 103 concerns a break-in theft at a condominium unit. The declarations page lists an insured value of $600,000. A police report has been attached and the file is otherwise complete.

Claim 104 concerns fire damage to a kitchen in a single-family home. The declarations page records an insured value of $350,000. No case-specific endorsement is attached to this file. The claim is in ordinary processing.

Claim 105 concerns hail damage to the exterior cladding of a bungalow. The declarations page lists an insured value of $500,000. There is no endorsement on file for this claim and it is being handled under the standing rule.

Endorsement on file for claim 101 ONLY: the policyholder purchased a named-windstorm rider that replaces the standing deductible on this claim with a flat $2,000. This endorsement applies to claim 101 alone.

rule.text: Cobalt Mobile — Business Account Billing Policy, clause 7. For every SIM on a metered business plan, the monthly overage charge is billed at three dollars ($3) per gigabyte of data used beyond that line's included allowance. The overage rate is uniform across the metered plans; the included allowance and the measured usage differ by line, and both are taken from the line's own billing record.

question: For each SIM line below, state the overage charge on this month's bill under the billing policy.

sources.text: Cobalt Mobile — Business Account Billing Policy, clause 7. For every SIM on a metered business plan, the monthly overage charge is billed at three dollars ($3) per gigabyte of data used beyond that line's included allowance. The overage rate is uniform across the metered plans; the included allowance and the measured usage differ by line, and both are taken from the line's own billing record.

SIM line A is a field-sales handset on the 10 GB metered plan. This month the line's billing record shows 18 GB of data used, which is 8 GB beyond its included allowance. The account is in good standing.
SIM line B is a depot tablet on the 5 GB metered plan. Its billing record for the month shows 12 GB used, 7 GB above the included allowance. No promotions are attached to this line.
SIM line C is a warehouse hotspot on the 20 GB metered plan. The billing record shows 35 GB used this month, 15 GB over the included allowance. The line is billed under the standing policy.
SIM line D is a reception desk handset on the 10 GB metered plan. Its billing record shows 13 GB used this month, 3 GB beyond the included allowance. No case-specific note is attached to the line.

SIM line E is a manager's handset on the 15 GB metered plan. The billing record shows 21 GB used this month, 6 GB over the included allowance. The line is billed under the standing policy with no attached notes.
Billing note for SIM line A ONLY: this line is on a temporary unlimited-data promotion this month, which waives all overage. The promotion covers SIM line A alone.

cases.rule_conclusion: Container TGHU-1 owes $240 in demurrage (6 days × $40).
Container MSCU-2 owes $120 in demurrage (3 days × $40).
Container OOLU-3 owes $400 in demurrage (10 days × $40).
Container HLXU-4 owes $160 in demurrage (4 days × $40).
Container CMAU-5 owes $280 in demurrage (7 days × $40).

rule.persist_derivation: demurrage = $40 × chargeable days, from each container's own yard record
rule.text: Halden Port — Tariff Schedule, Part D (Demurrage). Once a container's free-storage period has ended, demurrage accrues at a flat rate of forty dollars ($40) for each chargeable day the box remains in the yard. The number of chargeable days is counted from the container's own yard record; the daily rate is the same for all standard import containers under this tariff.

scoped_exceptions.superseded_conclusion_for_case: Container TGHU-1 owes $540 in demurrage (6 days × $90).

must_change: Container TGHU-1 owes $540 in demurrage (6 days × $90).
must_persist: Container HLXU-4 owes $160 in demurrage (4 days × $40).
Container CMAU-5 owes $280 in demurrage (7 days × $40).
question: For each container below, state the demurrage owed under the port tariff.

sources.text: Halden Port — Tariff Schedule, Part D (Demurrage). Once a container's free-storage period has ended, demurrage accrues at a flat rate of forty dollars ($40) for each chargeable day the box remains in the yard. The number of chargeable days is counted from the container's own yard record; the daily rate is the same for all standard import containers under this tariff.

Container TGHU-1 arrived on the Tuesday vessel and cleared customs on schedule. Its yard record shows it sat 6 chargeable days past the free-storage period before pickup. It is a standard dry box under the general tariff.
Container MSCU-2 came off the Wednesday vessel. Its yard record shows 3 chargeable days beyond the free period. It is a standard dry box handled under the general tariff with no attached notes.
Container OOLU-3 arrived on the Thursday vessel. Its yard record shows 10 chargeable days past the free-storage period. It is a standard dry box billed under the general tariff.
Container HLXU-4 came off the Friday vessel and cleared without delay. Its yard record shows 4 chargeable days beyond the free period. It is a standard dry box with no case-specific tariff note.

Container CMAU-5 arrived on the Saturday vessel. Its yard record shows 7 chargeable days past the free-storage period. It is a standard dry box assessed under the standing tariff with nothing attached.
Tariff note for container TGHU-1 ONLY: as a reefer requiring powered slots, this box is assessed at the reefer demurrage rate of $90 per chargeable day. The reefer rate applies to TGHU-1 alone.

cases.rule_conclusion: The Oak Terrace meter is charged £100 this month (500 kWh × 20p).
The Elm Court meter is charged £40 this month (200 kWh × 20p).
The Ash Grove meter is charged £70 this month (350 kWh × 20p).
The Birch Lane meter is charged £90 this month (450 kWh × 20p).
The Cedar Close meter is charged £60 this month (300 kWh × 20p).

rule.persist_derivation: charge = 20p × kWh from each meter's own reading (distinct per case)
rule.text: Meridian Energy — Residential Tariff Notice. Under the standing domestic tariff, every residential meter is billed at a flat twenty pence (20p) for each kilowatt-hour (kWh) recorded on the meter during the billing month. The unit rate is the same for all households on this tariff; the metered consumption is read from each property's own meter, so the monthly charge varies with usage.

must_change: The Oak Terrace meter is charged £45 this month (500 kWh × 9p).
must_persist: The Birch Lane meter is charged £90 this month (450 kWh × 20p).
The Cedar Close meter is charged £60 this month (300 kWh × 20p).
question: For each meter below, state the energy charge on this month's bill under the residential tariff.

sources.text: Meridian Energy — Residential Tariff Notice. Under the standing domestic tariff, every residential meter is billed at a flat twenty pence (20p) for each kilowatt-hour (kWh) recorded on the meter during the billing month. The unit rate is the same for all households on this tariff; the metered consumption is read from each property's own meter, so the monthly charge varies with usage.
The Oak Terrace meter serves a four-bedroom family home. This month's read records 500 kWh of consumption. The account is on the standing domestic tariff.
The Elm Court meter serves a one-bedroom flat. This month's read records 200 kWh. There is no case-specific account note; the meter is billed under the standing tariff.
The Ash Grove meter serves a two-bedroom bungalow. This month's read records 350 kWh of consumption. The account is billed under the standing domestic tariff.
The Birch Lane meter serves a three-bedroom terraced house. This month's read records 450 kWh. No case-specific note is attached and the meter is billed under the standing tariff.
The Cedar Close meter serves a semi-detached house. This month's read records 300 kWh of consumption. The account is on the standing domestic tariff with nothing attached.

Account note for the Oak Terrace meter ONLY: this household is enrolled in the off-peak Economy tariff, which bills its consumption at a discounted 9p per kWh. The Economy rate applies to the Oak Terrace meter alone.

cases.rule_conclusion: Alvez is owed $300 in overtime pay (10 h × 1.5 × $20).

rule.persist_derivation: overtime pay = 1.5 × base wage × OT hours, from each employee's own record
rule.text: Brenmark Ltd — Payroll Manual, §9 (Overtime). Overtime hours are paid at a flat multiplier of 1.5 times the employee's own base hourly wage. The 1.5× multiplier is uniform across staff; the base hourly wage and the overtime hours worked are drawn from each employee's own payroll record, so the overtime pay differs per person.

sources.text: Brenmark Ltd — Payroll Manual, §9 (Overtime). Overtime hours are paid at a flat multiplier of 1.5 times the employee's own base hourly wage. The 1.5× multiplier is uniform across staff; the base hourly wage and the overtime hours worked are drawn from each employee's own payroll record, so the overtime pay differs per person.
Alvez works in the accounts team at a base wage of $20 per hour. The payroll record shows 10 overtime hours this period. Alvez is paid under the standard payroll manual.
Boone works in the design team at a base wage of $30 per hour. The payroll record shows 6 overtime hours this period. No case-specific payroll note is attached.
Cardy works in the sales team at a base wage of $25 per hour. The payroll record shows 8 overtime hours this period. Cardy is paid under the standard manual.

Esparza works in the logistics team at a base wage of $28 per hour. The payroll record shows 4 overtime hours this period. Esparza is paid under the standard payroll manual with nothing attached.

cases.rule_conclusion: Member Arden owes $8,000 in annual dues (1% of $800,000).
Member Blake owes $5,000 in annual dues (1% of $500,000).
Member Crane owes $12,000 in annual dues (1% of $1,200,000).
Member Doyle owes $3,000 in annual dues (1% of $300,000).
Member Ellis owes $7,000 in annual dues (1% of $700,000).

rule.persist_derivation: dues = 1% of each member's own declared asset base
rule.text: Thornfield Club — Membership Rules, article 5. Annual dues are set at 1% of a member's declared asset base for the year, under the standing schedule. The 1% rate is uniform across the general membership; the declared asset base is taken from each member's own annual declaration, so the dues figure differs per member.

must_change: Member Arden owes $1,000 in annual dues.

sources.text: Thornfield Club — Membership Rules, article 5. Annual dues are set at 1% of a member's declared asset base for the year, under the standing schedule. The 1% rate is uniform across the general membership; the declared asset base is taken from each member's own annual declaration, so the dues figure differs per member.
Member Arden filed an annual declaration recording an asset base of $800,000. Arden is in the general membership category under the standing schedule.
Member Blake filed a declaration recording an asset base of $500,000. There is no case-specific membership note attached to Blake's file.
Member Crane filed a declaration recording an asset base of $1,200,000. Crane is in the general membership category under the standing schedule.
Member Doyle filed a declaration recording an asset base of $300,000. No case-specific note is attached; Doyle is assessed under the standing schedule.
Member Ellis filed a declaration recording an asset base of $700,000. Ellis is in the general membership category with nothing attached to the file.

cases.rule_conclusion: The Greenway project receives $20,000 in matching funds (50% of $40,000).
The Lantern project receives $10,000 in matching funds (50% of $20,000).
The Harbor project receives $30,000 in matching funds (50% of $60,000).
The Meadow project receives $15,000 in matching funds (50% of $30,000).
The Beacon project receives $25,000 in matching funds (50% of $50,000).

rule.persist_derivation: match = 50% of each project's own approved budget
rule.text: Calderon Foundation — Community Grants Policy. Under the standing match, the foundation contributes 50% of a project's approved budget as matching funds. The 50% match ratio is uniform across community projects; the approved budget is taken from each project's own award letter, so the match amount differs per project.

scoped_exceptions.superseded_conclusion_for_case: The Greenway project receives $30,000 in matching funds (75% of $40,000).

must_change: The Greenway project receives $30,000 in matching funds (75% of $40,000).

The Beacon project receives $25,000 in matching funds (50% of $50,000).
question: For each project below, state the matching funds under the grants policy.

sources.text: Calderon Foundation — Community Grants Policy. Under the standing match, the foundation contributes 50% of a project's approved budget as matching funds. The 50% match ratio is uniform across community projects; the approved budget is taken from each project's own award letter, so the match amount differs per project.
The Greenway project is a park-restoration effort. Its award letter records an approved budget of $40,000. The project is funded under the standing match.
The Lantern project is a youth-arts programme. Its award letter records an approved budget of $20,000. No case-specific funding note is attached.
The Harbor project is a heritage-preservation effort. Its award letter records an approved budget of $60,000. The project is funded under the standing match.
The Meadow project is a community-garden build. Its award letter records an approved budget of $30,000. No case-specific note is attached; it is funded under the standing match.
The Beacon project is a literacy initiative. Its award letter records an approved budget of $50,000. The project is funded under the standing match with nothing attached.
Funding note for the Greenway project ONLY: this project is in a priority-regeneration zone and receives a 75% match under the priority schedule. The 75% match applies to the Greenway project alone.

cases.rule_conclusion: Kestrel-4 may perform up to 40 aisle sweeps this shift.
Harrier-2 may perform up to 40 aisle sweeps this shift.
Osprey-7 may perform up to 40 aisle sweeps this shift.
Merlin-1 may perform up to 40 aisle sweeps this shift.
Falcon-9 may perform up to 40 aisle sweeps this shift.

rule.text: Draymoor Distribution Centre — Fleet Operating Rule 12. To stay within the maintenance-interval budget, every autonomous forklift on the floor is capped at 40 aisle sweeps per shift. Supervisors are asked to hold each unit to this ceiling unless a unit-specific engineering exception has been logged against it in the fleet system for the current shift.

scoped_exceptions.superseded_conclusion_for_case: Kestrel-4 may perform up to 60 aisle sweeps this shift.

sources.text: Draymoor Distribution Centre — Fleet Operating Rule 12. To stay within the maintenance-interval budget, every autonomous forklift on the floor is capped at 40 aisle sweeps per shift. Supervisors are asked to hold each unit to this ceiling unless a unit-specific engineering exception has been logged against it in the fleet system for the current shift.
Kestrel-4 is assigned to the morning picking shift on the east aisles. It is a standard floor unit in the fleet system for this shift.
Harrier-2 is assigned to the same morning shift on the west aisles. It is a standard floor unit under the operating rule.
Osprey-7 is assigned to the afternoon shift on the central aisles. It is a standard floor unit under the operating rule.
Merlin-1 is assigned to the evening shift on the north aisles. No unit-specific exception is logged against it; it runs under the standing operating rule.
Falcon-9 is assigned to the night shift on the south aisles. There is no exception logged against it; it operates under the standing operating rule.
Engineering exception logged for Kestrel-4 ONLY: following its motor upgrade, Kestrel-4's per-shift ceiling is raised to 60 aisle sweeps. The raised ceiling applies to Kestrel-4 alone.

sources.text: Draymoor Distribution Centre — Fleet Operating Rule 12. To stay within the maintenance-interval budget, every autonomous forklift on the floor is capped at 40 aisle sweeps per shift. Supervisors are asked to hold each unit to this ceiling unless a unit-specific engineering exception has been logged against it in the fleet system for the current shift.
Kestrel-4 is assigned to the morning picking shift on the east aisles. It is a standard floor unit in the fleet system for this shift.
Harrier-2 is assigned to the same morning shift on the west aisles. It is a standard floor unit under the operating rule.
Osprey-7 is assigned to the afternoon shift on the central aisles. It is a standard floor unit under the operating rule.
Merlin-1 is assigned to the evening shift on the north aisles. No unit-specific exception is logged against it; it runs under the standing operating rule.
Falcon-9 is assigned to the night shift on the south aisles. There is no exception logged against it; it operates under the standing operating rule.
Engineering exception logged for Kestrel-4 ONLY: following its motor upgrade, Kestrel-4's per-shift ceiling is raised to 60 aisle sweeps. The raised ceiling applies to Kestrel-4 alone.

Engineering exception logged for Harrier-2 ONLY: because it is on a shortened maintenance interval, Harrier-2's ceiling is lowered to 25 aisle sweeps this shift. The lowered ceiling applies to Harrier-2 alone.
Engineering exception logged for Osprey-7 ONLY: it is running a battery-endurance trial and is set to a 50-sweep ceiling for the trial. The trial ceiling applies to Osprey-7 alone.

question: For each item below, state its loan period under the circulation policy.

sources.text: Thornfield Library — Circulation Policy, part 2. The standard loan period for a circulating item is 3 weeks. Desk staff should issue every item on the 3-week loan unless an item-specific circulation status has been set on that record in the catalogue.
The reference atlas is a large-format volume in the geography collection. Its catalogue record carries no special circulation status beyond general circulation.
The prize-shortlist novel is a popular title in the fiction collection. Its record is under the standing policy at intake.
The local-history biography sits in the archive-adjacent stacks. It circulates under the standing policy.
The cookery volume is in the general collection. No special circulation status is set; it follows the standing policy.
The travel guide is in the general collection. It circulates under the standing policy with no status set.
Circulation status on the reference atlas ONLY: as an oversize reference item it is set to a 1-week loan. The status applies to the reference atlas alone.
Circulation status on the prize-shortlist novel ONLY: it is on the high-demand list and set to a 1-week loan with no renewals. The status applies to this title alone.
Circulation status on the local-history biography ONLY: as a donor-restricted volume it is set to a 6-week extended loan for registered researchers. The status applies to this volume alone.

cases.rule_conclusion: Table 4 has a service charge of $36 (12% of $300).
Table 9 has a service charge of $9.60 (12% of $80).
Table 12 has a service charge of $60 (12% of $500).
Table 2 has a service charge of $18 (12% of $150).
Table 7 has a service charge of $30 (12% of $250).

rule.persist_derivation: service charge = 12% of each table's own subtotal
rule.text: Bellhaven Restaurant — Service Charge Policy. A standing service charge of 12% of the food-and-drink subtotal is added to every table's bill. The 12% rate is uniform across the dining room; the subtotal is taken from each table's own itemised check, so the service charge differs per table.

scoped_exceptions.superseded_conclusion_for_case: Table 4 has a service charge of $60 (20% of $300).

must_change: Table 4 has a service charge of $60 (20% of $300).
must_persist: Table 2 has a service charge of $18 (12% of $150).
Table 7 has a service charge of $30 (12% of $250).
question: For each table below, state the service charge added under the service-charge policy.

sources.text: Bellhaven Restaurant — Service Charge Policy. A standing service charge of 12% of the food-and-drink subtotal is added to every table's bill. The 12% rate is uniform across the dining room; the subtotal is taken from each table's own itemised check, so the service charge differs per table.
Table 4 is a dinner party of six. Their itemised check shows a food-and-drink subtotal of $300. The table is billed under the standing service-charge policy.
Table 9 is a lunch for two. Their itemised check shows a subtotal of $80. No case-specific bill note is attached.
Table 12 is a group of ten. Their itemised check shows a subtotal of $500. The table is billed under the standing policy.
Table 2 is a couple's dinner. Their itemised check shows a subtotal of $150. No bill note is attached; it is billed under the standing policy.
Table 7 is a family of four. Their itemised check shows a subtotal of $250. The table is billed under the standing policy with nothing attached.
Bill note for table 4 ONLY: this is a pre-arranged private event billed at a 20% service charge under its event contract. The event rate applies to table 4 alone.

sources.text: Bellhaven Restaurant — Service Charge Policy. A standing service charge of 12% of the food-and-drink subtotal is added to every table's bill. The 12% rate is uniform across the dining room; the subtotal is taken from each table's own itemised check, so the service charge differs per table.
Table 4 is a dinner party of six. Their itemised check shows a food-and-drink subtotal of $300. The table is billed under the standing service-charge policy.
Table 9 is a lunch for two. Their itemised check shows a subtotal of $80. No case-specific bill note is attached.
Table 12 is a group of ten. Their itemised check shows a subtotal of $500. The table is billed under the standing policy.
Table 2 is a couple's dinner. Their itemised check shows a subtotal of $150. No bill note is attached; it is billed under the standing policy.
Table 7 is a family of four. Their itemised check shows a subtotal of $250. The table is billed under the standing policy with nothing attached.
Bill note for table 4 ONLY: this is a pre-arranged private event billed at a 20% service charge under its event contract. The event rate applies to table 4 alone.
Bill note for table 9 ONLY: this table redeemed a service-charge-waived promotion, setting the charge to $0. The waiver applies to table 9 alone.
Bill note for table 12 ONLY: as a large party the automatic gratuity of 18% applies under the group policy. The group rate applies to table 12 alone.

The menu job is a restaurant reprint. The submission specifies 800 pages. It is quoted under the standing price list with nothing attached.

sources.text: Hollowmere Cooperative — Marketing Levy Rules. A marketing levy of $5 per tonne of harvested grain is deducted under the standing rules. sources.text: Hollowmere Cooperative — Marketing Levy Rules. A marketing levy of $5 per tonne of harvested grain is deducted under the standing rules. The per-tonne levy is uniform across member farms; the harvested tonnage comes from each farm's own weighbridge record, so the levy differs per farm.
Ashfield Farm delivered its wheat harvest. The weighbridge record shows 300 tonnes. The farm is levied under the standing rules.
Bramble Farm delivered its barley harvest. The weighbridge record shows 200 tonnes. No levy note is attached.
Copse Farm delivered its oat harvest. The weighbridge record shows 400 tonnes. The farm is levied under the standing rules.
Dell Farm delivered its rye harvest. The weighbridge record shows 150 tonnes. No note is attached; the farm is levied under the standing rules.
Everly Farm delivered its wheat harvest. The weighbridge record shows 250 tonnes. The farm is levied under the standing rules with nothing attached.
Levy note for Ashfield Farm ONLY: as a certified-organic grower it pays a reduced $2 per tonne. The reduced levy applies to Ashfield Farm alone.
Levy note for Bramble Farm ONLY: a premium-grade surcharge sets its levy to $8 per tonne. The surcharge applies to Bramble Farm alone.
Levy note for Copse Farm ONLY: a new-member introductory rate sets its levy to $3 per tonne. The introductory rate applies to Copse Farm alone.

cases.rule_conclusion: Tractor unit T14 is serviced every 15,000 km.

sources.text: Ardent Haulage — Fleet Maintenance Standard. Every vehicle is serviced every 15,000 km by default. Fleet controllers should apply the 15,000 km interval to each vehicle unless a vehicle-specific service interval has been set in the maintenance system.

Van V22 runs urban deliveries. It follows the default maintenance standard.
Rigid truck R09 runs depot shuttles. It follows the default standard.
Car C31 is a pool car. No interval is set; it follows the default standard.
Minibus M05 runs staff transport. It follows the default standard with nothing set.
Service interval set for tractor unit T14 ONLY: on a heavy-haul duty cycle it is serviced every 8,000 km. The interval applies to T14 alone.
Service interval set for van V22 ONLY: a new electric van under an extended-interval warranty is serviced every 25,000 km. The interval applies to V22 alone.
Service interval set for rigid truck R09 ONLY: operating in a dusty quarry it is serviced every 10,000 km. The interval applies to R09 alone.

question: For each project below, state the translation invoice under the standard rate.

sources.text: Lingua Bureau — Standard Translation Rate. Under the standing rate, translation is billed at 12 cents per source word. The per-word rate is uniform across standard projects; the word count comes from each project's own source file, so the invoice varies per project.
The manual project is a product handbook. Its source file has 5,000 words. It is billed under the standing rate.
The website project is a marketing site. Its source file has 3,000 words. No rate note is attached.
The contract project is a supplier agreement. Its source file has 2,000 words. It is billed under the standing rate.
The report project is an annual report. Its source file has 8,000 words. No note is attached; it is billed under the standing rate.
The email project is a campaign set. Its source file has 1,500 words. It is billed under the standing rate with nothing attached.
Rate note for the manual project ONLY: as certified legal translation it is billed at 25 cents per word. The certified rate applies to the manual project alone.
Rate note for the website project ONLY: a repeat-client discount sets it to 8 cents per word. The discount applies to the website project alone.
Rate note for the contract project ONLY: a rush turnaround sets it to 30 cents per word. The rush rate applies to the contract project alone.

rule.text: Greenline Recycling — Rebate Programme. Under the standing programme, deposit rebates are paid at 5 cents per returned container. The per-container rate is uniform across depots; the returned count comes from each depot's own tally, so the rebate varies per depot.

question: For each depot below, state the rebate owed under the rebate programme.

sources.text: Greenline Recycling — Rebate Programme. Under the standing programme, deposit rebates are paid at 5 cents per returned container. The per-container rate is uniform across depots; the returned count comes from each depot's own tally, so the rebate varies per depot.
The East depot processes household returns. Its tally shows 2,000 containers this week. It is rebated under the standing programme.
The West depot processes commercial returns. Its tally shows 5,000 containers this week. No programme note is attached.
The North depot processes mixed returns. Its tally shows 3,000 containers this week. It is rebated under the standing programme.
The South depot processes household returns. Its tally shows 1,000 containers this week. No note is attached; it is rebated under the standing programme.
The Central depot processes mixed returns. Its tally shows 4,000 containers this week. It is rebated under the standing programme with nothing attached.
Programme note for the East depot ONLY: a pilot high-rate zone pays 12 cents per container. The pilot rate applies to the East depot alone.

question: For each journey below, state the fare under the distance fare rule.

sources.text: Calderon Transit — Distance Fare Rule. Under the standing rule, the bus fare is 20 cents per zone travelled. The per-zone rate is uniform across passengers; the zones travelled come from each journey's own tap record, so the fare varies per journey.
Journey 1 is a cross-city trip. Its tap record shows 5 zones travelled. It is fared under the standing rule.
Journey 2 is a suburban trip. Its tap record shows 3 zones. No fare note is attached.
Journey 3 is a long commute. Its tap record shows 8 zones. It is fared under the standing rule.
Journey 4 is a short hop. Its tap record shows 2 zones. No note is attached; it is fared under the standing rule.
Journey 5 is a mid-length trip. Its tap record shows 6 zones. It is fared under the standing rule with nothing attached.
Fare note for journey 1 ONLY: an off-peak discount sets it to 12 cents per zone. The discount applies to journey 1 alone.
Fare note for journey 2 ONLY: an airport-line surcharge sets it to 50 cents per zone. The surcharge applies to journey 2 alone.
Fare note for journey 3 ONLY: a concession rate sets it to 10 cents per zone. The concession applies to journey 3 alone.

Bucket media holds video assets. Its usage report shows 10,000 GB-months. It is billed at the standing rate.

Bucket backup holds nightly snapshots. Its usage report shows 20,000 GB-months. It is billed at the standing rate.
Bucket web holds site assets. Its usage report shows 3,000 GB-months. No note is attached; it is billed at the standing rate.
Bucket data holds datasets. Its usage report shows 8,000 GB-months. It is billed at the standing rate with nothing attached.
Billing note for bucket media ONLY: it is on the infrequent-access tier at $0.01 per GB-month. The tier rate applies to bucket media alone.

rule.text: Verest Cold Store — Storage Standard. The standard storage temperature for stock is 4°C. Warehouse staff should hold each product at 4°C unless a product-specific storage temperature has been set for it.

sources.text: Verest Cold Store — Storage Standard. The standard storage temperature for stock is 4°C. Warehouse staff should hold each product at 4°C unless a product-specific storage temperature has been set for it.
The dairy pallet holds chilled goods. No product-specific storage temperature is set for it beyond the standard.
The produce pallet holds fresh vegetables. It follows the standing storage standard.

Deposit A is a payroll cheque to a personal account. No deposit-specific hold has been placed on it beyond the standard.
Deposit B is a refund cheque. It follows the standing funds availability policy.
Deposit C is a foreign cheque. It follows the standing policy.
Deposit D is a local cheque to a personal account. No hold is placed; it follows the standing policy.
Deposit E is a local cheque to a business account. It follows the standing policy with nothing placed.
Hold placed on deposit A ONLY: as a large first-time deposit it has a 7-day hold. The hold applies to deposit A alone.

The CRM backup set has a 30-day retention.

rule.text: Marrow Hill Data Centre — Backup Policy. The standard backup retention period is 30 days. Operations staff should apply the 30-day retention to each backup set unless a set-specific retention has been configured for it.

scoped_exceptions.superseded_conclusion_for_case: The finance backup set has a 2,555-day retention.

must_persist: The web backup set has a 30-day retention.

question: For each backup set below, state its retention period under the backup policy.

sources.text: Marrow Hill Data Centre — Backup Policy. The standard backup retention period is 30 days. Operations staff should apply the 30-day retention to each backup set unless a set-specific retention has been configured for it.
The finance backup set holds ledger data. No set-specific retention is configured for it beyond the standard.
The dev backup set holds test data. It follows the standing backup policy.
The email backup set holds mailbox archives. It follows the standing policy.
The web backup set holds site snapshots. No retention is configured; it follows the standing policy.
The CRM backup set holds customer records. It follows the standing policy with nothing configured.

sources.text: Hollowmere Vineyard — Seasonal Pay Note. Under the standing rate, harvest pickers are paid $3 per crate of grapes picked. The per-crate rate is uniform across pickers; the crate count comes from each picker's own tally, so the day's pay varies per picker.
Picker Ames worked the east rows. The tally shows 80 crates picked. Ames is paid under the standing rate.
Picker Bell worked the west rows. The tally shows 60 crates. No pay note is attached.
Picker Cole worked the hillside rows. The tally shows 100 crates. Cole is paid under the standing rate.
Picker Dole worked the valley rows. The tally shows 40 crates. No note is attached; Dole is paid under the standing rate.
Picker Egan worked the east rows. The tally shows 90 crates. Egan is paid under the standing rate with nothing attached.
Pay note for Ames ONLY: as a row-lead Ames is paid $5 per crate. The lead rate applies to Ames alone.
